EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines down across the city and parts of the county. At least one tree fell onto an unoccupied vehicle. Winds were estimated by spotters at around 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms containing hail and damaging thunderstorm winds moved through central Indiana on the evening of October 18, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a moist atmosphere, slow movement of convection and continued development of new convection resulted in heavy rainfall across much of northern Indiana. Radar estimated 2 to 5 inches of rain in many areas; however, localized flooding was reported in several areas and only isolated reports of flash flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State route 218 was impassable and closed in southern Cass county due to flowing water on the road. A spotter in the area reported 4 inches of rain falling in one hour just southwest of Walton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms containing hail and damaging thunderstorm winds moved through central Indiana on the evening of October 18, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down near Fayetteville along highway 158 due to damaging thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought severe thunderstorms to much of south central Indiana. Thunderstorm wind damage occurred, mostly in the form of downed trees and power lines. The severe thunderstorms also dropped large hail over much of the area, causing some damage to vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought severe thunderstorms to much of south central Indiana. Thunderstorm wind damage occurred, mostly in the form of downed trees and power lines. The severe thunderstorms also dropped large hail over much of the area, causing some damage to vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast across the Tennessee Valley, bringing a period of snow, sleet, and freezing rain. The highest accumulations of snow and sleet were along and north of Interstate 64. From 1 to 3 inches fell in those areas. An observer near Petersburg in Pike County measured 3 inches of snow and sleet. An observer in extreme northern Vanderburgh County measured about an inch of snow and sleet. In Evansville, several hours of freezing rain left a coating of ice up to two tenths of an inch thick. The ice was mainly on trees and vehicles. East of Evansville, temperatures were generally too warm for the mixed precipitation to cause hazardous conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of sleet caused slippery going for a short time. According to a spotter in Poseyville, ice pellets fell quickly enough to cause portions of roadways and sidewalks to get a little slick. The affected area was mostly along and west of a Princeton to Poseyville line. The precipitation was caused by a southerly low-level wind flow of warmer air combined with a weak 500 mb trough over the Plains.

A cold front with strong upper level support collided with a very moist air mass over the lower Ohio Valley. Widespread severe thunderstorms resulted, along with three confirmed tornadoes in south central Indiana. The severe storms and tornadoes produced property damage, widespread areas with downed trees and power lines, and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed. Half inch hail fell.

A cold front with strong upper level support collided with a very moist air mass over the lower Ohio Valley. Widespread severe thunderstorms resulted, along with three confirmed tornadoes in south central Indiana. The severe storms and tornadoes produced property damage, widespread areas with downed trees and power lines, and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-3 tornado touched down near Bull Creek Road, and was on the ground intermittently before lifting near the end of Fulton Road, just short of the Ohio River. The greatest damage occurred about 10 miles east northeast of Charlestown, where a recently constructed two story home was blown off its basement and destroyed. Two large barns, a stable, a silo, and four vehicles were also damaged or destroyed on the same property. Ten other homes were damaged, with four of them receiving severe damage. Five barns and several other outbuildings were destroyed. The tornado left scour marks for nearly one half mile through corn and bean fields between Bull Creek Road and Church Road just after its initial touchdown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with strong upper level support collided with a very moist air mass over the lower Ohio Valley. Widespread severe thunderstorms resulted, along with three confirmed tornadoes in south central Indiana. The severe storms and tornadoes produced property damage, widespread areas with downed trees and power lines, and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The door of a house was blown out about four miles northeast of Ferdinand, near the start of the tornado path. A barn was damaged about a half mile south of Kyana, near the end of the damage path. Over 100 large oak trees were snapped off or uprooted in the Ferdinand Forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with strong upper level support collided with a very moist air mass over the lower Ohio Valley. Widespread severe thunderstorms resulted, along with three confirmed tornadoes in south central Indiana. The severe storms and tornadoes produced property damage, widespread areas with downed trees and power lines, and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed. Public estimated an 80 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along portions of the Wabash River in central Indiana due to heavy rain and snow melt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred along the Wabash River in Tippecanoe County near Lafayette due to heavy rain and snow melt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued along portions of the Wabash River from February into March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wabash River in Vigo County remained above flood from February through March, dipping below flood stage only briefly during the month.  Crests were over 6 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms containing hail and damaging thunderstorm winds moved through central Indiana on the evening of October 18, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging thunderstorm winds caused trees to fall around Vigo County, especially in the Terre Haute area.  Over 7,400 people were without power.  One tree fell onto a house and damaged the roof and caused a leak over a bedroom.  Another tree crashed through a carport and damaged a pickup truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The White River flooded yet again in late March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White River flooded in Owen County.  Crests were nearly 7 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet, remnants of a morning complex of storms from Iowa, and increasing instability all combined to create favorable conditions for damaging winds and isolated supercells. One supercell in particular had a storm motion towards the southeast when the line it was associated with moved mainly eastward. This supercell did produce a tornado in Marshall county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 10 to 12 inch tree branch was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms moved across northwest Indiana May 15th, producing significant wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a pickup truck on State Road 149, north of US Route 6, killing the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Powerful storms developed over southern Lake Michigan and moved south across northwest Indiana during the evening hours of August 15th.
Powerful, severe storms moved across northwest Indiana during the afternoon and evening hours of August 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated gust to 60 mph south of Route 30 and east of Route 41.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary, unstable atmosphere and strong winds aloft all allowed for another round of severe storms to develop across a good portion of northern Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered reports of trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heavy rainfall event around mid-month sent the Patoka River above flood stage. The ground was already wet from abundant rainfall in December and early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Princeton, where flood stage is 18 feet, the Patoka River crested at 20.48 feet on the 21st. This resulted in minor flooding of lowland fields and woods.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prefrontal trough moved across Michigan producing storms that layed out several outflow boundaries that progressed south into Indiana. These boundaries interacted with a high CAPE/low shear environment to set the stage for pulse thunderstorms. Several wet microbursts developed and produced areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in and around the city of Lagrange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonable warm and moist air interacted with a powerful cold front to bring several small lines of thunderstorms. While the majority of the stronger winds were not able to reach the ground, some wind gusts in the 60 to over 80 mph range were noted across parts of Northern Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof was peeled off trailer home. Damage is estimated at $5000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms containing hail and damaging thunderstorm winds moved through central Indiana on the evening of October 18, 2007.
Heavy rains in late December and January led to prolonged high water along the Wabash River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wabash River in Parke County remained high through most of January.  Crests were as high as 12 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved through central Indiana on the afternoon of 3 April 2007.  The storms produced mostly large hail, with isolated damaging wind.

A warm front moving north from central Indiana produced large scale deep ascent on the north side of the front. This led to the development of thunderstorms, some of which became severe across parts of northern Indiana.
Severe thunderstorms moved through central Indiana on the afternoon of 3 April 2007.  The storms produced mostly large hail, with isolated damaging wind.

A cold front with strong upper level support collided with a very moist air mass over the lower Ohio Valley. Widespread severe thunderstorms resulted, along with three confirmed tornadoes in south central Indiana. The severe storms and tornadoes produced property damage, widespread areas with downed trees and power lines, and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Powerful, severe storms moved across northwest Indiana during the afternoon and evening hours of August 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inch diameter tree limb blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front brought some severe thunderstorms to south central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large pine trees were downed along State Highway 60.
